scenario: Caught on camera stealing a $5 sandwich, clear HD footage, confessed on the spot.
plea: Plead guilty, pay a fine, no jail time.
risk: Go to trial — the footage is airtight, expect a conviction and 30 days.
reason: Overwhelming evidence; the plea avoids jail.

scenario: Accused of shoplifting, but the only witness was across the store and not wearing glasses.
plea: Plead to a lesser charge with a small fine.
risk: Go to trial — witness identification is weak.
reason: Weak evidence; trial has a real chance of acquittal.

scenario: Pulled over with a blood alcohol of 0.11, breathalyser calibrated yesterday, three officers present.
plea: Plead guilty, license suspension, mandatory classes.
risk: Go to trial — the reading is hard to challenge.
reason: Strong scientific evidence; plea minimises consequences.

scenario: Accused of assault, but the alleged victim has changed their story three times.
plea: Plead to disorderly conduct, community service.
risk: Go to trial — credibility issues may sink the case.
reason: Unreliable witness; trial is worth the gamble.

scenario: Found with stolen goods in your car, your fingerprints on the items, and a signed confession.
risk: Go to trial — the confession alone will convict.
reason: Confession plus physical evidence; plea is the safe move.

scenario: Accused of vandalism, but the security camera was broken that night and no witnesses came forward.
risk: Go to trial — no evidence places you at the scene.
reason: No evidence; trial likely ends in acquittal.

scenario: Charged with fraud after bank records, emails, and a co-conspirator's testimony all point to you.
risk: Go to trial — the paper trail is devastating.
reason: Multiple corroborating sources; plea and cooperation reduce sentence.

scenario: Accused of burglary, but the only evidence is a fuzzy photo that could be anyone in a hoodie.
risk: Go to trial — the photo is not identifiable.
reason: Ambiguous evidence; reasonable doubt is likely.

scenario: Drug possession charge; officers found the substance in your pocket during a lawful search, lab confirmed it.
risk: Go to trial — the search was lawful and the lab is conclusive.
reason: Conclusive evidence; diversion is the best outcome.

scenario: Accused of hit-and-run, but the paint on your bumper does not match the victim's car.
plea: Plead to a lesser charge, pay fines.
risk: Go to trial — the forensic evidence does not match.
reason: Forensic mismatch creates reasonable doubt.

scenario: Embezzlement charge with a signed ledger, wire transfer records, and a video of you taking the cash.
risk: Go to trial — the evidence is overwhelming.
reason: Video plus financial records; plea is the only rational choice.

scenario: Accused of arson, but the fire marshal's report says the cause was electrical, not intentional.
risk: Go to trial — the official report supports accident.
reason: Expert report favours accident; trial should clear you.

scenario: Caught selling counterfeit goods, with inventory, sales records, and undercover buyer testimony.
plea: Plead guilty, forfeit goods, pay fines.
risk: Go to trial — the records and testimony will convict.
reason: Multiple evidence streams; plea avoids heavier penalties.

scenario: Accused of robbery, but you were at work 20 miles away with 10 coworkers who can confirm.
risk: Go to trial — the alibi is strong.
reason: Solid alibi; trial should result in acquittal.

scenario: Charged with tax evasion; the IRS has five years of unreported income documents with your signature.
plea: Plead guilty, pay back taxes plus penalties.
risk: Go to trial — signed documents are hard to beat.
reason: Documentary evidence is conclusive; settle with the IRS.

scenario: Accused of trespassing, but the property had no signs and the gate was open and unlocked.
risk: Go to trial — no notice means no trespass.
reason: No notice of prohibited entry; trial should acquit.

scenario: Weapon possession charge; the gun was found in your bag at a checkpoint, your name on the bag.
risk: Go to trial — possession is clear-cut.
reason: Possession is undeniable; plea may avoid added charges.

scenario: Accused of blackmail, but the alleged letters were never recovered and the accuser has a grudge.
risk: Go to trial — no physical evidence exists.
reason: No evidence; a motivated accuser is not enough.

scenario: Charged with identity theft; police found 15 stolen credit cards in your apartment, all with your prints.
risk: Go to trial — the cards and prints are damning.
reason: Physical evidence is overwhelming; plea and cooperate.

scenario: Accused of loitering, but you were waiting for a bus at a designated stop with a valid ticket.
risk: Go to trial — you had a legitimate reason to be there.
reason: Legitimate purpose; trial should dismiss the charge.

scenario: Caught driving without a licence, pulled over for speeding, name check confirmed no licence.
plea: Plead guilty, pay fines, apply for licence.
risk: Go to trial — the records are clear.
reason: Records confirm the offence; plea is straightforward.

scenario: Accused of harassment, but all messages were sent from a spoofed number, not your phone.
plea: Plead to a lesser offence, counselling.
risk: Go to trial — the spoofing creates reasonable doubt.
reason: Messages not from your device; doubt is strong.

scenario: Charged with receiving stolen property; the serial numbers match, the seller is a known fence.
risk: Go to trial — you bought in good faith, but the price was suspicious.
reason: Suspicious circumstances make the trial risky; plea is safer.

scenario: Accused of disorderly conduct, but the only witness is the officer who has filed three false reports before.
plea: Plead to a noise violation, small fine.
risk: Go to trial — the officer's credibility is in question.
reason: Officer has a history of false reports; trial is worth it.

scenario: Drug trafficking charge; police have wiretaps, a confession, and three kilograms seized from your car.
risk: Go to trial — the wiretaps and drugs are undeniable.
reason: Multiple evidence types; cooperation is the only leverage.

scenario: Accused of public intoxication, but the breathalyser was not administered and no field test was done.
risk: Go to trial — no test means no proof.
reason: No test results; the case rests on officer opinion.

scenario: Charged with forgery; the signature was analysed by an expert who says it is yours.
risk: Go to trial — expert handwriting analysis is persuasive.
reason: Expert testimony is strong; plea avoids a harsher sentence.

scenario: Accused of breaking a restraining order, but the order had expired two days before the alleged incident.
risk: Go to trial — the order was not in effect.
reason: Expired order means no violation; trial should clear you.

scenario: Charged with perjury; your testimony directly contradicts security footage that was playing in court.
risk: Go to trial — the footage was shown in open court.
reason: Contradiction on the record; plea is the only sensible option.

scenario: Accused of nuisance, but the complaint is from a neighbour over a one-time barbecue that ended by 8 p.m.
risk: Go to trial — one brief event is not a nuisance.
reason: Single isolated incident; unlikely to sustain a nuisance claim.
